---
title: "Hands-on: Quarto in 1 Hour"
subtitle: "From a blank document to a PDF, a chart, and slides — all from your data"
lang: en
---

> 🌐 **Versi Bahasa Indonesia:** [baca dalam Bahasa Indonesia](../hands-on.qmd)

::: {.callout-warning}
## Before the session (install these FIRST)
Installing R/RStudio/TinyTeX can take ~30–60 minutes, so **do it before the workshop** so
our hour goes to *practice*, not downloading.

1. **R** — <https://cloud.r-project.org/>
2. **RStudio Desktop** — <https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/> (ships with **Quarto**)
3. In the RStudio **Terminal**: `quarto install tinytex`
4. In the RStudio **Console**: `install.packages(c("rmarkdown","knitr","dplyr","ggplot2"))`
5. *(Optional, for Python examples)* `pip install jupyter pandas matplotlib` + the `reticulate` package.

Done? Great. In the session we'll just verify with `quarto check`.
:::

## Agenda (~60 min)

| Time | Activity |
|---|---|
| ⏱️ 5′ | **0.** Check your setup |
| ⏱️ 10′ | **1.** Your first document (HTML + PDF) |
| ⏱️ 15′ | **2.** Prose + code + data |
| ⏱️ 10′ | **3.** The *reproducible* payoff (change the data) |
| ⏱️ 10′ | **4.** Slides from the same source |
| ⏱️ 10′ | **5.** Q&A & next steps |

Download the sample data: **`data/data_kemiskinan_jateng.csv`** (from the workshop repo).
Put it in your working folder under a `data/` subfolder.

---

## ⏱️ 0 · Check your setup (5′)

Create a new working folder, open it in RStudio, then in the **Terminal**:

```bash
quarto check
```

Look for **(✓)** for Quarto and LaTeX. (If using Python: also for Jupyter.)

## ⏱️ 1 · Your first document (10′)

Create `hello.qmd`:

````markdown
---
title: "Hello Quarto"
format: html
---

## Welcome
A normal paragraph: **bold**, *italic*, `code`. A list:

- one
- two
````

Click **Render** (or `quarto render hello.qmd`). Then change `format: html` → `format: pdf`
and render again. **Same source, two outputs.** 🎉

## ⏱️ 2 · Prose + code + data (15′)

The heart of Quarto: code runs at render time and its results flow into the document.
Create `analysis.qmd` (pick R **or** Python):

::: {.panel-tabset}
### R

````markdown
---
title: "Central Java Poverty"
format: html
---

```{{r}}
library(dplyr); library(ggplot2)
df <- read.csv("data/data_kemiskinan_jateng.csv")
df23 <- df[df$tahun == 2023, ]
```

Mean poverty in 2023 = **`r round(mean(df23$kemiskinan), 2)`%**.

```{{r}}
df |> group_by(tahun) |> summarise(rata = mean(kemiskinan)) |>
  ggplot(aes(tahun, rata)) + geom_line() + geom_point() +
  labs(x = "Year", y = "Poverty (%)")
```
````

### Python

````markdown
---
title: "Central Java Poverty"
format: html
jupyter: python3
---

```{{python}}
import pandas as pd, matplotlib.pyplot as plt
df = pd.read_csv("data/data_kemiskinan_jateng.csv")
df23 = df[df["tahun"] == 2023]
print(f"Mean 2023: {df23['kemiskinan'].mean():.2f}%")
```

```{{python}}
df.groupby("tahun")["kemiskinan"].mean().plot(marker="o")
plt.xlabel("Year"); plt.ylabel("Poverty (%)"); plt.show()
```
````
:::

Render. Notice: the **inline number** and the **chart** both come from the data — no copy-paste.

## ⏱️ 3 · The *reproducible* payoff (10′) {#sec-repro}

The "aha" moment:

1. Open `data/data_kemiskinan_jateng.csv`.
2. Change **Kota Semarang 2023** from `4.58` → `24.58`. **Save.**
3. **Re-render** `analysis.qmd`.

The mean and the chart change instantly. One data edit → the whole document follows.

::: {.callout-important}
## Must know: `quarto render`, not just *preview*
The live preview (on `localhost`) **does not watch data files**. After editing **data**,
run **`quarto render`** in the Terminal and open the result — or edit-and-save a `.qmd` to
make the preview refresh. *(This is the most common gotcha.)*
:::

## ⏱️ 4 · Slides from the same source (10′)

No rewriting needed. Edit the header of `analysis.qmd` to add **two slide formats**:

````markdown
---
title: "Central Java Poverty"
format:
  revealjs: default     # HTML slides (screen/web)
  beamer:               # PDF slides (projector)
    aspectratio: 169
---
````

Then:

```bash
quarto render analysis.qmd --to revealjs   # -> analysis.html
quarto render analysis.qmd --to beamer     # -> analysis.pdf
```

Each `##` heading becomes a slide; your chart appears on a slide — still from the same data.

## ⏱️ 5 · Done & next steps (10′)

You just: wrote a document, ran analysis inside it, proved *reproducibility*, and made
slides — **all from one source**.

Keep going:

- 📘 **Book "Quarto untuk Skripsi"** (complete, Chapter I–V, ready templates; Indonesian) —
  <https://firmanhadi21.github.io/thesis-with-quarto/>
- 📦 **Thesis templates (R & Python) + defense slides** — in the book's repo.
- 📑 **Quarto documentation** — <https://quarto.org/docs/guide/>
- 🔖 **Citations via Zotero** — <https://quarto.org/docs/authoring/citations.html>
